metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: English Language Arts 8 builds students' skills in reading, writing, speaking, listening, and representing through engagement with a wide range of literary and informational texts. Students explore how stories and texts are socially and culturally constructed, how language shapes identity, and how critical thinking helps them become engaged citizens. First Peoples texts, perspectives, and oral traditions are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: An integrated study of reading, writing, speaking, listening, and representing through diverse literary and informational texts, developing critical literacy and creative communication skills.
metadata.course_structure_note: ELA 8 is a single integrated course (not modular). Competencies address both Comprehend & Connect and Create & Communicate.

big_ideas.statement: Language and text can be a source of creativity and joy.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reading and writing are not just functional skills — they offer pleasure, aesthetic experience, and the joy of creative play with ideas and language.

big_ideas.statement: Exploring stories and other texts helps us understand ourselves and make connections to others and to the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Literature and storytelling are tools for empathy, self-discovery, and understanding the human condition across cultures and time.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their worldviews and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: A reader's cultural background, personal experience, and values all shape how they interpret a text — meaning is not fixed but constructed.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every text reflects the context in which it was made — the author's identity, the time period, the culture, and the intended audience all shape what gets said and how.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critical literacy — the ability to analyze texts for bias, purpose, and perspective — is essential for navigating a complex information landscape.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Access information and ideas for diverse purposes from a variety of sources and evaluate their relevance, accuracy, and reliability.
i_can_statements: I can find information from a variety of sources (books, websites, videos, interviews) for different purposes.
I can evaluate whether a source is relevant, accurate, and reliable.
I can explain why the source of information matters.

competencies.raw: Apply appropriate strategies to comprehend written, oral, and visual texts, guide inquiry, and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can use strategies like predicting, questioning, visualizing, and summarizing to understand what I read, hear, or view.
I can choose the strategy that fits best for the type of text I'm working with.
I can extend my thinking beyond the text by making inferences and asking new questions.

competencies.raw: Synthesize ideas from a variety of sources to build understanding.
i_can_statements: I can connect ideas from multiple texts to build a deeper understanding of a topic.
I can explain how different sources add to, challenge, or change my understanding.
I can pull together ideas from different texts to form my own conclusion.

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate how different features, forms, and genres of texts reflect different purposes, audiences, and messages.
i_can_statements: I can identify the form and genre of a text and explain how they suit its purpose.
I can describe how an author's choices about form (poem, story, article) affect how the message is communicated.
I can compare how the same idea is communicated differently in two different text forms.

competencies.raw: Think critically, creatively, and reflectively to explore ideas within, between, and beyond texts.
i_can_statements: I can analyze ideas within a text and think about what they mean beyond the story.
I can make connections between two texts and explain what that connection reveals.
I can reflect on how a text changed or challenged my thinking.

competencies.raw: Recognize and identify the role of personal, social, and cultural contexts, values, and perspectives in texts.
i_can_statements: I can identify the perspective behind a text and how it shapes the message.
I can recognize how a character's or author's social background influences their worldview.
I can identify whose perspective might be missing from a text.

competencies.raw: Recognize how language constructs personal, social, and cultural identity.
i_can_statements: I can explain how the words a writer chooses reflect their cultural or personal identity.
I can identify examples of how language creates or reinforces social norms.
I can discuss how the language used in a text positions readers to think in a certain way.

competencies.raw: Construct meaningful personal connections between self, text, and world.
i_can_statements: I can make a text-to-self connection and explain what it reveals about me or the text.
I can make a text-to-world connection and use it to deepen my understanding.
I can explain how a story relates to something happening in the world today.

competencies.raw: Respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways.
i_can_statements: I can write a personal response to a text, explaining how it affected me.
I can create a response to a text in a different form (e.g., artwork, dramatic reading, rewrite).
I can critically analyze a text and support my interpretation with evidence.

competencies.raw: Recognize how literary elements, techniques, and devices enhance and shape meaning.
i_can_statements: I can identify literary devices (metaphor, simile, imagery, foreshadowing) and explain their effect.
I can analyze how characterization, setting, and plot structure contribute to meaning.
I can explain how a specific literary choice by the author changes how we feel about a character or event.

competencies.raw: Recognize an increasing range of text structures and how they contribute to meaning.
i_can_statements: I can identify the structure of a text (cause-effect, compare-contrast, problem-solution, chronological) and explain how it organizes meaning.
I can describe how a text's structure helps the reader follow the argument or narrative.
I can compare two texts with different structures and explain how each serves its purpose.

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the role of story, narrative, and oral tradition in expressing First Peoples perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can explain what oral tradition is and why it is important in First Peoples cultures.
I can describe how a First Peoples story communicates values, history, or knowledge.
I can approach First Peoples texts with respect and an understanding of their cultural context.

competencies.raw: Develop an awareness of the protocols and ownership associated with First Peoples texts.
i_can_statements: I can explain that First Peoples oral stories and texts may have protocols around sharing and use.
I can recognize that some stories belong to specific communities and require permission to retell.
I can discuss why it matters to respect the intellectual and cultural ownership of Indigenous texts.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Exchange ideas and viewpoints to build shared understanding and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can share my ideas in discussions and build on what others say.
I can listen respectfully to different viewpoints and explain how they extend my thinking.
I can collaborate with others to develop ideas I couldn't reach on my own.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes to plan, develop, and create engaging and meaningful literary and informational texts for a variety of purposes and audiences.
i_can_statements: I can plan my writing by considering my audience, purpose, and key ideas.
I can draft, revise, and edit my writing to improve clarity and impact.
I can create texts that are appropriate for their intended audience and purpose.

competencies.raw: Assess and refine texts to improve their clarity, effectiveness, and impact according to purpose, audience, and message.
i_can_statements: I can review my own writing and identify where it could be clearer or more effective.
I can give and receive specific, constructive feedback on writing.
I can revise my work based on feedback to improve its impact.

competencies.raw: Use an increasing repertoire of conventions of Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation.
i_can_statements: I can apply Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation conventions in my writing.
I can proofread my work to catch and correct errors.
I can explain the purpose of specific grammar or punctuation rules.

competencies.raw: Use and experiment with oral storytelling processes.
i_can_statements: I can tell a story orally using appropriate pacing, expression, and detail.
I can experiment with oral storytelling conventions such as repetition, dialogue, and imagery.
I can adapt a written story for oral delivery.

competencies.raw: Select and use appropriate features, forms, and genres according to audience, purpose, and message.
i_can_statements: I can choose the right form (poem, essay, story, report) for my purpose and audience.
I can explain why I chose a particular form and how it suits my message.
I can experiment with different genres to find which works best for my topic.

competencies.raw: Transform ideas and information to create original texts.
i_can_statements: I can take an idea or information from a source and transform it into an original piece of writing.
I can write in my own voice, not just summarize what I've read.
I can create a text in a new form that expresses my own understanding or perspective.

areas.name: Story/Text — Forms, Functions, and Genres
areas.raw: Narrative, exposition, report; various literary and thematic categories.
topics: Narrative texts: fiction, short stories, novels
Expository texts: articles, reports, essays
Poetry: lyric, narrative, concrete
Literary genres: fantasy, adventure, mystery, biography, humour
Purposes of text: to entertain, inform, persuade, explore, express

areas.name: Story/Text — Literary Elements and Devices
areas.raw: Characterization, character types, story structures (linear, cyclical, iterative), setting; sensory detail (imagery, sound devices), figurative language (metaphor, simile, hyperbole).
topics: Characterization: direct and indirect, character types (protagonist, antagonist, static, dynamic)
Story structure: linear (beginning, middle, end), cyclical, iterative
Setting: time, place, atmosphere
Imagery: sensory language creating vivid pictures
Sound devices: alliteration, assonance, onomatopoeia
Figurative language: metaphor, simile, personification, hyperbole
Narrative structures in First Peoples texts (circular, iterative, cyclical)

areas.name: Story/Text — Visual and Graphic Texts
areas.raw: Layout, infographics, emoticons, icons, symbols, interactive visuals, hypertext, colour, illustration styles.
topics: Layout: how design choices guide reading
Infographics: visual presentation of data and information
Icons, symbols, and emoticons as communication
Hypertext and interactive digital texts
Illustration styles: realism, cartoon, sketch, outline
Colour and its meaning in visual texts

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies, oral language strategies, metacognitive strategies, writing processes.
topics: Reading strategies: contextual clues, phonics, word structure, visualizing, questioning, predicting, previewing, summarizing, making inferences
Oral language strategies: focusing on speaker, asking clarifying questions, listening for specifics, expressing opinions, speaking with expression, staying on topic, taking turns
Metacognitive strategies: reflecting on thinking, questioning, goal setting, self-evaluating
Writing processes: generating ideas, planning, drafting, revising, editing, publishing

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Oral language features, multi-paragraphing, language usage and context, elements of style, syntax and sentence fluency, conventions.
topics: Oral language: tone, volume, inflection, pace, gestures
Paragraph structure: unity, development, coherence
Language and context: how context shapes word choice
Elements of style: diction, figurative language, tone, inclusive language, formality
Syntax: mixing simple, compound, and complex sentences
Grammar: pronoun use, subject-verb agreement, transitional words, avoiding run-ons and fragments
Conventions: punctuation, capitalization, quoting, Canadian spelling
Presentation techniques: choosing appropriate media for purpose and audience
